name: Heinie Manush
born: July 20, 1901 — Tuscumbia, AL (Helen Keller's hometown; German immigrant father)
hof: Class of 1964 (Veterans Committee). Alabama Sports HOF 1972. Michigan Sports HOF 1964.

stat_justification.con: .378 in 1928 (narrowly lost batting title to Goslin by 1 point). .378 in 1926 (won batting title, beating Ruth). Career .330 over 17 seasons. 2,524 hits. Led AL in hits twice (1928: 241, 1933: 221). Hit .300+ in 11 full seasons. Four 200-hit seasons. Ty Cobb taught him to bunt and hit line drives. Manush: 'No one ever gained a batting championship without confidence in his ability to hit.' Maximum contact.
stat_justification.pow: 13 HR in 1928. 110 career. SLG .575 earns the bonus. But 47 doubles and 20 triples that year — pure gap power. Career 491 doubles, 160 triples. Not a power hitter by design — a line-drive machine. Rating of 2.
stat_justification.spd: 16 SB in 1928 (career high). 114 career SB. 20 triples in 1928. Had real speed, especially early. But 64% SB success rate was mediocre, and speed declined with age. Rated above-average range in his prime. Rating of 2.
stat_justification.def: Led AL in fielding % (.992) and putouts in LF in 1928. Solid fielder. But analysts note below-average range and throwing arm, especially later. 'Essentially a DH playing before the DH existed' (This Day in Baseball). Rating of 1.
stat_justification.clu: .111 in only World Series (1933, Senators vs Giants). 2-for-18 with 0 RBI. Ejected from Game 4 for bumping umpire Charlie Moran after a called third strike. His temper cost his team. No clutch postseason moments. Rating of 0.

personality.leadership_style: The Firebrand. Manush was a fellow Southerner with Cobb's temper — fiery, competitive, physical. He led through intensity, not quiet authority. His battle with Goslin for the batting title became an annual $50 bet with a suit on the line. He was a fighter, not a diplomat. The kind of player who bumps umpires and doesn't apologize.
personality.temperament: Hot-tempered, passionate, combustible. SABR: 'Mastering the art of the line drive but unable to master his own temper.' The WS ejection for bumping umpire Moran after a called third strike is the defining moment — his rage cost his team in the only World Series he ever reached. But that same fire drove his .330 career average.
personality.work_ethic: Cobb's student. Learned to bunt, to hit line drives, to compete with ferocity under the greatest hitter who ever lived. Manush absorbed Cobb's relentless work ethic. His swing was a craft honed over years — compact, line-drive, lefty. He battled for playing time as a rookie against HOFers and won. Pure competitive will.
personality.lifestyle: German-American family man. One of 8 children (7 boys, 1 girl) of a German immigrant cabinet maker. Married Betty — three daughters (Shirley, Lillis, Sue). Betty died of cancer at 46 in 1949; Heinie never remarried. Picked up golf in 1935, won multiple city championships in Sarasota. Fought throat cancer, died in a nursing home at 69.
personality.era_adaptability: MODERATE. Manush's .330 career average and line-drive approach translate, but his lack of power and defensive limitations would hurt in modern baseball. He'd be a high-average corner outfielder with limited value beyond the bat — a one-tool player in a five-tool world.
personality.clubhouse_impact: HIGH but volatile. Manush's fire could inspire or disrupt. His annual betting rivalry with Goslin was beloved. He formed part of great outfields (Cobb/Heilmann/Manush in Detroit, Goslin/Manush in Washington). But his ejection from the World Series shows the cost of that fire.
personality.dark_side: The World Series ejection. In the only Fall Classic he ever reached (1933), Manush went 2-for-18 (.111) and got himself thrown out of Game 4 for bumping umpire Charlie Moran. His temper — the same fire that drove .330 for 17 years — betrayed him at the worst possible moment. He never got another chance. Also: Veterans Committee HOF pick means his candidacy is debated. Lower-tier HOFer by modern metrics.

chemistry_traits.desc: +1 CON innate. Learned to hit line drives under the greatest hitter in history. The Cobb school of hitting — bunt, slash, spray.
In September, +1 CON when within 10 points of the batting leader. Won the 1926 title going 6-for-9 on the final day. Lost 1928 by 1 point.
When paired with Goslin, annual rivalry: +1 CON for both. Loser buys winner a suit. Manush won the bet 5 of 8 times.
+1 intimidation. But 15% chance per game of losing temper — argument with umpire, -1 DEF, risk of ejection.
Converts 15% of fly outs into doubles. Manush's swing produced flat, screaming line drives to all fields.
In World Series, if Manush gets a called third strike, 30% chance he bumps the umpire and gets ejected. His fire was his gift and his curse.

preferred_locations.note: Cobb's student. Lived in the cage. The line-drive swing was art.
preferred_locations.note_extra: Source labeled 'Batting Cages / Practice Field' / affinity 'HIGH' — mapped to Practice Field/HIGH as the primary craft/preparation domain. Manush learned his swing under Ty Cobb and devoted himself to the mechanics of the line-drive approach. Consistent with the established position-craft and training-craft → Practice Field/HIGH mapping.

preferred_locations.note: Won multiple city golf championships in Sarasota after retirement.
preferred_locations.note_extra: Source labeled 'Golf Course' / affinity 'HIGH' — mapped to Community Events/HIGH as the post-career leisure-community identity domain. Golf became Manush's primary competitive and social outlet in retirement: he won multiple city championships in Sarasota from 1935 onward. The golf course as a competitive recreational community (tournaments, city championships, social rounds) maps to the Community Events domain rather than Practice Field (which encodes professional craft) or Hotel/Rest (which encodes passive rest). Consistent with Leever's trap shooting → Community Events/MEDIUM pattern, but elevated to HIGH because golf was Manush's defining post-career identity. First golf-course → Community Events/HIGH mapping in the set.

preferred_locations.note: Fiery but popular. The rivalry with Goslin was more friendship than feud.
preferred_locations.note_extra: Source labeled 'Clubhouse / Locker Room' / affinity 'HIGH' — mapped to Community Events/HIGH as the internal team-community social domain. The clubhouse is the primary communal space of the professional baseball team: rivalries (Goslin bet), outfield partnerships (Cobb/Heilmann/Manush, Goslin/Manush), team identity formation. This is distinct from Practice Field (craft) and from Hotel/Rest (private rest). Community Events encodes the social/communal dimension. Two Community Events/HIGH entries on this card (Golf Course and Clubhouse) — first card with two Community Events/HIGH entries. Prior cards had at most one HIGH per Community Events node.

preferred_locations.note: He visited often. Not by choice — usually being ejected or fined.
preferred_locations.note_extra: Source labeled 'Umpire's Office' / affinity 'HIGH' / note 'He visited often. Not by choice — usually being ejected or fined.' — preserved as an ironic abstract-penalty domain entry consistent with the set's pattern of encoding abstract/non-physical locations as affinity entries. HIGH is the source affinity and is preserved. The Umpire's Office is not a standard ILB node but an ironic encoding of Manush's chronic ejection and disciplinary record: the 1933 WS ejection, documented suspension risk in chemistry_traits.Southern Fire and chemistry_traits.The Ejection, and the broader pattern of umpire confrontations throughout his career. The irony is part of the card's identity — the man who bumped his way into infamy had a perverse HIGH affinity for the one place players most want to avoid.

preferred_locations.note: German-American community ties. Family man with deep Southern roots.
preferred_locations.note_extra: Source labeled 'Community Events' / affinity 'MEDIUM' — preserved as Community Events/MEDIUM encoding the social/cultural identity of German-American immigrant family life and Southern community roots. Secondary communal affinity, consistent with the established MEDIUM-as-background-community pattern.

Source labeled 'Bar / Nightlife' / affinity 'LOW' — mapped to Hotel/Rest/LOW as the avoidance-of-nightlife register. Consistent with prior Hotel/Rest/LOW nightlife-avoidance mappings (Lazzeri: 'couldn't afford Ruth's lifestyle'). Manush's LOW is less charged narratively — simply the absence of nightlife association rather than a medically or morally prescribed avoidance.

preferred_locations.note: Not a media darling. Overshadowed by bigger names his entire career.
preferred_locations.note_extra: Source labeled 'Spotlight / Media' / affinity 'LOW' — mapped to Media/Spotlight/LOW. First Media/Spotlight/LOW in the set. Prior Media/Spotlight entries have been HIGH (home-stage identity at a ballpark) or NONE (Lazzeri: active aversion to press rooms). LOW encodes a secondary, understated relationship with the media domain: present but marginal, chronically overshadowed by Cobb, Ruth, Heilmann, Goslin, and bigger names throughout his career. Manush hit .378 twice and lost a batting title by one point; he never became a media figure. Media/Spotlight/LOW as the register of the overlooked HOFer — consistent with art_direction.reference: 'The card of the HOFer nobody remembers.'

hot_triggers: Batting races — his fire ignited in September. Won 1926 title on final day. Lost 1928 by a single point.
Playing alongside great hitters — Cobb/Heilmann in Detroit, Goslin/Cronin in Washington
First full seasons with new teams — .334 rookie year, .378 after trade to Browns
cold_triggers: World Series pressure — .111 in only WS appearance (1933), ejected from Game 4
Follow-up seasons after big years — dropped 80 points from .378 to .298 in 1927
Losing playing time battles — struggled when not a clear starter
Temper eruptions — ejections, suspensions, loss of focus
momentum.pressure_response: SPLIT. In regular-season batting races: elite (.378 twice, won 1926 title on final day in doubleheader 6-for-9). In the World Series: catastrophic (.111, ejected). Manush was a regular-season warrior who melted in October. His temper, which fueled his hitting, destroyed his composure when the stakes were highest.

action_card_seeds.title: 6-for-9 on the Final Day
action_card_seeds.text: Your outfielder trails in the batting race going into the final day. He goes 6-for-9 in a doubleheader to pass the leader and win the title. The crowd storms the field.
action_card_seeds.origin: 1926: Manush trailed Babe Ruth entering the final day. He went 6-for-9 in a doubleheader to win the batting title at .378, beating Ruth's .372.

action_card_seeds.title: One Point Short
action_card_seeds.text: Your hitter finishes the season at .378 — one point behind the batting champion. The rival's final-game single on a ball the right fielder couldn't quite reach decided everything.
action_card_seeds.origin: 1928: Manush hit .378 but lost the title to Goose Goslin by one point. Goslin singled on the final play. Browns RF Beauty McGowan couldn't make the catch.

action_card_seeds.title: The $50 Suit
action_card_seeds.text: Two rival outfielders bet $50 and a new suit on who finishes with the higher batting average. Both players gain +1 CON from the rivalry. At season's end, the loser pays up with a handshake.
action_card_seeds.origin: Manush and Goslin made an annual bet after their 1928 batting race. $50 and a suit. Manush won 5 times, Goslin won 3. A rivalry built on mutual respect.

action_card_seeds.title: Bumping the Umpire
action_card_seeds.text: In the World Series, your fiery hitter gets called out on strikes. He bumps the umpire. Ejection. Your team loses the game and the Series momentum. His temper — the source of his greatness — costs everything.
action_card_seeds.origin: 1933 WS Game 4: Manush bumped umpire Charlie Moran after a called third strike. Ejected from his only World Series. Senators lost the Series to the Giants.

action_card_seeds.title: Cobb's Classroom
action_card_seeds.text: A young outfielder learns to hit under the greatest batter in history. The mentor teaches bunting, line drives, and fury. +2 CON permanently. But the student also inherits the mentor's temper.
action_card_seeds.origin: Manush learned to hit under Ty Cobb in Detroit (1923-27). Cobb taught him the line-drive swing. Both were fiery Southern temperaments. The apprentice absorbed the master's genius — and his rage.

action_card_seeds.title: The All-Hall Outfield
action_card_seeds.text: Your team fields an outfield of three future Hall of Famers simultaneously. +2 CON for all three. The outfield hits a combined .360.
action_card_seeds.origin: Detroit 1923-27: Ty Cobb (CF), Harry Heilmann (RF), Heinie Manush (LF). Three HOFers in one outfield — they combined for 17 batting titles.

action_card_seeds.title: Filet Mignon, Heinie Manush
action_card_seeds.text: On a train ride, a teammate discovers that your player's name perfectly mimics the rhythm of the train wheels. The entire dining car starts chanting his name. +2 team chemistry. The conductor is driven to distraction.
action_card_seeds.origin: Teammate Vance O'Brien ordered dinner on a train: 'Filet mignon that's for me, Heinie Manush, Heinie Manush, Heinie Manush.' The rhythmic name fit the train's clatter perfectly. Other passengers picked it up.

art_direction.face: Strong, angular, intense. 6'1" 200 lbs — a big man for the era. LH batting stance: compact, flat swing, the line-drive machine. The face of a man who's about to argue with someone, probably an umpire. German-American features, Southern intensity.
art_direction.attire: St. Louis Browns 1928 road grays or home whites. The forgotten franchise — the Browns were bad and overlooked. Manush was their best player and nobody noticed. Classic 1920s-30s baggy flannel.
art_direction.mood: Controlled fury. The swing: flat, vicious, the ball screaming on a line into the gap. Or the confrontation: Manush jawing at an umpire, cap tilted, finger pointing. This card should feel like coiled energy that can go two ways — a line drive or an ejection.
art_direction.style: Slightly rougher sepia than the refined Dickey/Gehringer cards. More grain, more shadow. The card of a brawler who happened to hit .330. Sportsman's Park in the background — half-empty, mid-1920s.
art_direction.reference: The card of the HOFer nobody remembers. The Veterans Committee pick. The guy who hit .378 twice and lost a batting title by one point. The guy who got ejected from his only World Series. This card is baseball's beautiful contradiction.
